subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
questions.question: An object moves with constant acceleration. On an acceleration-time graph, the shape of the line is
questions.hint: Constant acceleration means the value of acceleration does not change with time.
questions.explanation: If acceleration is constant, its value is the same at every instant. On an acceleration-time graph, constant value appears as a horizontal line. A sloped line would indicate changing acceleration, and a parabola would indicate non-linear change.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
source_trace.content_anchor: For a uniformly accelerated motion, acceleration is constant with time. Hence, acceleration time-graph will be a straight line parallel to the time axis

explanations_tiered.definition: An acceleration-time graph plots acceleration on the vertical axis versus time on the horizontal axis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For uniform (constant) acceleration, the acceleration does not change, so the graph is a horizontal line at the value of \(a\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you always speed up by the same amount each second (like +3 m/s every second), your acceleration stays flat — a horizontal line on the graph.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Constant acceleration means \(a(t) = \text{constant}\). On an \(a\) vs \(t\) graph, this is a horizontal line. The slope of this line is zero.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For uniform acceleration, \(a = dv/dt = \text{constant}\). The graph \(a(t)\) is a horizontal line. The area under this line gives the change in velocity: \(\Delta v = a \Delta t\).
reasoning_steps: Recognize that constant acceleration means the numerical value of acceleration does not vary with time.
On a graph with time on x-axis, a constant y-value gives a horizontal line.
misconceptions: A horizontal line on acceleration-time graph means zero acceleration only if the line is at y=0.
The shape of position-time graph (parabola) is often confused with acceleration-time graph shape.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Constant acceleration → horizontal line on \(a\)-\(t\) graph.

glossary_entry.definition: A graph with acceleration on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis.

flashcard.front: What shape is the acceleration-time graph for constant acceleration?
flashcard.back: A horizontal line. The acceleration value does not change with time.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does the acceleration-time graph look like for constant acceleration?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A horizontal line. The acceleration does not change with time, so the graph is flat.

questions.glossary_definition: A graph with acceleration on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis.
questions.teacher_note: Contrast with velocity-time graph (sloped line) and position-time graph (parabola).
questions.learning_objective_tag: Draw graphs of position-time, velocity-time and acceleration-time graph
questions.workbook_section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
questions.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Constant acceleration → horizontal line on \(a\)-\(t\) graph.

section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
question: The graph of velocity versus time for an object with uniform acceleration is a straight line. The slope of this line gives the object's
hint: Slope on a velocity-time graph equals acceleration.
explanation: On a velocity-time graph, the slope \((v_2 - v_1)/(t_2 - t_1)\) is the definition of average acceleration. For uniform acceleration, this slope is constant and equals the instantaneous acceleration. Displacement is given by the area under the line, not the slope.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
source_trace.content_anchor: The slope of the velocity-time graph gives the acceleration of the particle

explanations_tiered.definition: A velocity-time graph plots velocity on the y-axis versus time on the x-axis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The slope (steepness) of a velocity-time graph equals acceleration. For uniform acceleration, the line is straight and the slope is constant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a velocity-time line gets steeper, the acceleration is larger. Slope = rise (change in velocity) over run (change in time) = acceleration.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Mathematically, slope = \(\frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = a_{av}\). For uniform acceleration, \(a_{av} = a\) and the slope is constant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous acceleration \(a = dv/dt\) is the derivative of the velocity function. On a \(v\) vs \(t\) graph, this derivative equals the slope of the tangent line, which for a straight line is the same at all points.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of acceleration: change in velocity divided by time.
On a graph, "change in y over change in x" is the slope.
Therefore, slope of v-t graph = acceleration.
misconceptions: Confusing slope (acceleration) with area under the curve (displacement).
Thinking a steeper line means faster speed — it means faster change in speed (larger acceleration).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: v-t graph slope = acceleration. Area = displacement.

glossary_entry.definition: A graph with velocity on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis.

flashcard.front: What physical quantity is represented by the slope of a velocity-time graph?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Acceleration. The slope = \(\Delta v / \Delta t\), which is the definition of acceleration.

glossary_definition: A graph with velocity on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis.
teacher_note: Emphasize distinction: slope = acceleration, area = displacement.
learning_objective_tag: Draw graphs of position-time, velocity-time and acceleration-time graph
workbook_section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
solution_manual_note: Answer C. Slope of v-t graph = acceleration. Area gives displacement.

subject: Physics
question: A student says: "If I walk 10 m east and then 10 m west, my position has changed by 20 m." This reasoning is incorrect because

options.A: Position does not depend on direction
options.B: Displacement is the net change, not the total distance
options.C: Position is always measured from the starting point only
options.D: Westward motion cancels eastward motion in position

hint: Position change is final minus initial, not the sum of path lengths.
explanation: The student has confused position change (displacement) with total distance traveled. Displacement = final position − initial position = 0 m, not 20 m. Option B correctly identifies the error. Option D is partially true but not the precise definition.

source_trace.content_anchor: displacement is the shortest distance between the two positions

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is the change in position (final minus initial), not the total path length traveled.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The student added distances (10 + 10) instead of subtracting final minus initial (0 − 0 = 0). Displacement can be zero even when distance is large.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Walking 10 steps forward then 10 steps back leaves you exactly where you started. Your position didn't change — displacement is zero, not 20.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Displacement \(\Delta s = s - s_o\). Starting at origin, east +10 m, west −10 m: final position = 0. \(\Delta s = 0 - 0 = 0\) m. The student incorrectly used total distance.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The error arises from conflating scalar (distance) and vector (displacement) quantities. Position is a vector relative to origin; displacement is the vector difference, not the sum of path segments.
reasoning_steps: Distance traveled is 20 m, but displacement is 0 m.
misconceptions: Thinking displacement equals total distance traveled is incorrect; displacement is the net change.
Believing that moving back and forth always adds to displacement.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Displacement = final position − initial position. Zero if return to start.

glossary_entry.definition: Change in position, final minus initial.

flashcard.front: A person walks 10 m east then 10 m west. Is the displacement 20 m? Why?
flashcard.back: No, displacement = 0 m. Displacement is final − initial position; returning to start gives zero, not total distance.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is displacement zero after walking 10 m east and 10 m west?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Displacement = final position − initial position = 0 m, because you return to start. Distance traveled is 20 m, but displacement is net change.

glossary_definition: Change in position, final minus initial.
revision_note: Displacement = final − initial. Return to start → zero.
teacher_note: Use number line to show that +10 then −10 returns to zero.
learning_objective_tag: Describe the difference between distance and displacement
solution_manual_note: Answer B. Displacement is net change (0 m), not total distance (20 m).

subject: Physics
question: A car moves along a straight line such that its position as a function of time is \(s(t) = 2t^2 + 3t\) (with \(s\) in meters, \(t\) in seconds). The displacement of the car between \(t = 1.0\,\mathrm{s}\) and \(t = 3.0\,\mathrm{s}\) is
explanation: \(s(3) = 2(9) + 3(3) = 18 + 9 = 27\,\mathrm{m}\); \(s(1) = 2(1) + 3(1) = 2 + 3 = 5\,\mathrm{m}\); displacement = \(27 - 5 = 20\,\mathrm{m}\). Option A subtracts incorrectly, B gives \(s(3)\) only, D gives \(s(3) + s(1)\).

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is the change in position: final position minus initial position.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Given \(s(t)\), find positions at the two times and subtract. The quadratic term \(2t^2\) indicates uniform acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Plug the times into the formula to find where the car is at each moment, then find how far it moved from start to finish.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement is \(\int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) dt\), but here directly using position function \(s(t)\): \(\Delta s = s(t_2) - s(t_1)\).
reasoning_steps: Substitute \(t = 3.0\) into \(s(t)\) to find final position.
misconceptions: Using \(s(3)\) alone as displacement (ignoring initial position).
Adding positions instead of subtracting.

glossary_entry.definition: Change in position over a time interval.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A car moves as \(s(t)=2t^2+3t\). Displacement \(t=1\) to \(t=3\)?

glossary_definition: Change in position over a time interval.
teacher_note: Check that students compute both positions correctly before subtracting.
learning_objective_tag: Calculate the total displacement given the position as a function of time

subject: Physics
question: A car travels 100 km at 50 km/h and then another 100 km at 100 km/h. The average speed for the entire trip is
hint: Average speed = total distance / total time, not the arithmetic mean of speeds.
explanation: Total distance = 200 km. Time for first segment = \(100/50 = 2.0\) h, second = \(100/100 = 1.0\) h, total time = 3.0 h. Average speed = \(200/3 = 66.7\) km/h. Option C is the incorrect arithmetic mean \((50+100)/2 = 75\).

source_trace.content_anchor: total distance travelled / total time taken

explanations_tiered.definition: Average speed is total distance traveled divided by total time taken.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Unlike average velocity, average speed does not consider direction. For trips with constant speeds on each segment, you must weight by time, not simply average the speeds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drive 1 hour at 50 km/h and 1 hour at 100 km/h, average is 75. But here distances are equal, not times, so the slower segment takes longer and pulls the average down.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Calculate each time: \(t_1 = d_1/v_1 = 100/50 = 2\) h, \(t_2 = 100/100 = 1\) h. Total time 3 h, total distance 200 km, so \(200/3 = 66.7\) km/h.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The harmonic mean (not arithmetic mean) applies when distances are equal: \(v_{av} = \frac{2}{1/v_1 + 1/v_2} = \frac{2}{1/50 + 1/100} = 66.7\) km/h.
worked_solution: Step 3: Total time = 3.0 h, total distance = 200 km
reasoning_steps: Divide total distance by total time.
misconceptions: Averaging the two speeds \((50+100)/2 = 75\) is wrong because the times are not equal.
Assuming equal time intervals when distances are equal.

glossary_entry.definition: Total distance traveled divided by total time taken.

flashcard.front: 100 km at 50 km/h then 100 km at 100 km/h. Average speed?
flashcard.back: 66.7 km/h. Total time = 3 h, total distance = 200 km, \(200/3 = 66.7\). Not the arithmetic mean (75).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 100 km at 50 km/h then 100 km at 100 km/h. Average speed?

glossary_definition: Total distance traveled divided by total time taken.
teacher_note: Classic trap: students average the speeds. Show that times differ.
learning_objective_tag: Solve problems related to the average velocity
solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(t_1=2\) h, \(t_2=1\) h, total 3 h, 200 km / 3 h = 66.7 km/h. Not 75.

subject: Physics
question: The speedometer of a car reads 80 km/h at a particular instant. This reading represents the car's
hint: A speedometer shows magnitude of velocity at that moment, not direction.
explanation: A speedometer gives the magnitude of instantaneous velocity (speed) at that instant, not direction. Instantaneous velocity includes direction, but the speedometer only shows magnitude. Average speed/velocity would require a time interval.

source_trace.content_anchor: The magnitude of the instantaneous velocity of a moving car is the reading of the speedometer

explanations_tiered.definition: Instantaneous speed is the magnitude of instantaneous velocity at a single moment.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A speedometer measures instantaneous speed, not direction, so it does not give velocity (which requires direction).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Your car's speedometer tells you how fast you are going right now, but not which way (north/south). That's speed, not velocity.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Instantaneous velocity \(\vec{v}\) has both magnitude and direction. A speedometer displays only the magnitude \(|\vec{v}|\), called instantaneous speed.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous speed \(v = |ds/dt|\) is the absolute value of the derivative of position with respect to time, whereas instantaneous velocity \(\vec{v} = d\vec{s}/dt\) includes sign/direction.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that a speedometer gives a reading at a single instant (not averaged over time).
It provides magnitude only, not direction.
misconceptions: Thinking speedometer gives average speed (it gives instantaneous).
Confusing speed with velocity (speedometer does not show direction).

flashcard.front: What does a car's speedometer measure?
flashcard.back: Instantaneous speed — the magnitude of instantaneous velocity at that moment.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does a speedometer show average or instantaneous speed?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Instantaneous speed. It gives the magnitude of velocity at that exact moment.

teacher_note: Point out that a GPS can show direction and speed, but a traditional speedometer is speed only.
learning_objective_tag: Define instantaneous and average velocity of a body in motion
solution_manual_note: Answer C. Speedometer gives instantaneous speed (magnitude only).

subject: Physics
question: A car moving east at 20 m/s applies brakes and comes to rest in 5.0 seconds. The average acceleration of the car is
hint: Acceleration = change in velocity / time. Slowing down gives negative acceleration in the direction of motion.
explanation: \(a_{av} = (v - v_o)/\Delta t = (0 - 20)/5.0 = -4.0\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\). The negative sign indicates acceleration opposite to the eastward velocity (deceleration). Option D incorrectly states west as direction without the sign convention consistency.

explanations_tiered.definition: Average acceleration is the change in velocity divided by the time interval.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When an object slows down, its acceleration is opposite to its velocity direction. Here, east is positive, so the negative acceleration is west.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you're going east and hit the brakes, you slow down. That's negative acceleration (or deceleration) because your speed is decreasing.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(a_{av} = \Delta v / \Delta t = (0 - 20)/5.0 = -4.0\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\). The negative sign means acceleration is westward (opposite to eastward motion).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Acceleration is a vector. If east is chosen as +x, then \(\vec{a} = -4.0\hat{i}\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\). The magnitude is \(4.0\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\) westward.
reasoning_steps: Interpret negative sign as opposite direction.
misconceptions: Thinking deceleration means acceleration is always negative regardless of sign convention.
Forgetting to include sign from velocity change.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Slowing down: acceleration opposite to velocity.

glossary_entry.definition: Negative acceleration relative to the direction of motion; speed decreases.

flashcard.front: A car going 20 m/s east stops in 5.0 s. What is average acceleration?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Car from 20 m/s east to stop in 5.0 s. Average acceleration?

glossary_definition: Negative acceleration relative to the direction of motion; speed decreases.
teacher_note: Emphasize that negative sign depends on chosen positive direction.

concept_map_nodes.target: Negative change in velocity over time

subject: Physics
question: The instantaneous acceleration of an object is defined as the limit of the average acceleration as
hint: Instantaneous quantities are limits as time interval shrinks to zero.
explanation: Instantaneous acceleration is \(a = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}\), the derivative of velocity with respect to time. This is the same pattern as instantaneous velocity (\(\lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \Delta s/\Delta t\)).

source_trace.content_anchor: as the interval of time \(\Delta t\) gets smaller and smaller, i.e., as \(\Delta t\) approaches 0

explanations_tiered.definition: Instantaneous acceleration is the acceleration at a specific instant, the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: By taking the limit \(\Delta t \to 0\), we remove the averaging effect and obtain the exact acceleration at one moment.
explanations_tiered.beginner: To know your exact acceleration at one instant, you look at a super tiny slice of time — the limit as the time chunk goes to zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Instantaneous acceleration \(a = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{dv}{dt}\). The time interval must approach zero, not displacement or velocity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mathematically, \(a(t) = dv/dt\) is the derivative of the velocity function. The limit process \(\Delta t \to 0\) defines the derivative.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of any instantaneous quantity.
It involves making the time interval very small (limit \(\Delta t \to 0\)).
misconceptions: Using \(\Delta s \to 0\) (that's for instantaneous velocity but with position).
Thinking \(\Delta v \to 0\) defines acceleration (that would give zero).

glossary_entry.definition: The acceleration at a specific instant, the derivative of velocity with respect to time.

flashcard.front: How is instantaneous acceleration defined mathematically?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the mathematical definition of instantaneous acceleration?

glossary_definition: The acceleration at a specific instant, the derivative of velocity with respect to time.
teacher_note: Link to slope of tangent on v-t graph.
learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between instantaneous acceleration and average acceleration
solution_manual_note: Answer B. Instantaneous acceleration = limit as \(\Delta t \to 0\) of \(\Delta v/\Delta t\).

subject: Physics
section: 2.4 Equations of motion with constant acceleration
question: A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at \(2.0\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\). The distance traveled in the first 6.0 seconds is
explanation: With \(v_o = 0\), \(s = \frac{1}{2}at^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2.0)(6.0)^2 = 0.5 \times 2.0 \times 36 = 36\,\mathrm{m}\). Option A gives \(at\), B gives \(a t^2/4\), C gives \(at^2/3\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.4 Equations of motion with constant acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: For uniformly accelerated motion starting from rest, displacement is \(s = \frac{1}{2}at^2\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: With \(v_o = 0\), the \(v_o t\) term disappears, leaving a simple quadratic relationship.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you start from a stop and accelerate steadily, the distance you cover grows with the square of the time.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equation \(s = v_o t + \frac{1}{2}at^2\) reduces to \(s = \frac{1}{2}at^2\) when \(v_o=0\). Here \(a=2.0\), \(t=6.0\), so \(s = 36\) m.

formula_analysis.name: Displacement from rest under uniform acceleration

misconceptions: Using \(s = at\) gives 12 m (speed after 6 s, not distance).
Forgetting to square time gives \(s = \frac{1}{2}at = 6\) m.

glossary_entry.definition: Equation relating displacement, initial velocity, acceleration, and time.

flashcard.front: Car accelerates from rest at 2.0 m/s² for 6.0 s. Distance?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Rest to 2.0 m/s² for 6.0 s. Distance?

glossary_definition: Equation relating displacement, initial velocity, acceleration, and time.
teacher_note: Check that students square time correctly.
learning_objective_tag: Use appropriate equations of motion to solve motion-related problems
workbook_section: 2.4 Equations of motion with constant acceleration

subject: Physics
question: A police car moves at \(20\,\mathrm{m/s}\) chasing a thief's car moving at \(15\,\mathrm{m/s}\) in the same direction. The velocity of the police car relative to the thief's car is
hint: Relative velocity in same direction: \(v_{AB} = v_A - v_B\).
explanation: Relative velocity of police (A) with respect to thief (B) is \(v_{AB} = v_A - v_B = 20 - 15 = 5\,\mathrm{m/s}\). Option D incorrectly adds the speeds (approach case).

source_trace.content_anchor: \(v_{AB} = v_A - v_B\) when moving in the same direction

explanations_tiered.definition: Relative velocity of object A with respect to B is \(\vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When two objects move in the same direction, the relative velocity is the difference of their speeds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you're running at 5 m/s and your friend runs at 4 m/s in the same direction, you move away at 1 m/s relative to them.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(v_{AB} = v_A - v_B = 20 - 15 = 5\,\mathrm{m/s}\). The police car approaches the thief at 5 m/s relative to the thief.
reasoning_steps: Apply relative velocity formula: subtract slower from faster.

variables.meaning: velocity of A relative to B

misconceptions: Adding speeds (20+15=35) is wrong unless they move toward each other.
Subtracting in wrong order (15-20=-5) gives magnitude but sign indicates direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Velocity of an object as measured from another moving reference frame.

flashcard.front: Police 20 m/s, thief 15 m/s same direction. Relative velocity of police to thief?
flashcard.back: \(20 - 15 = 5\,\mathrm{m/s}\). Police closes at 5 m/s relative to thief.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Same direction: police 20 m/s, thief 15 m/s. Police relative to thief?

glossary_definition: Velocity of an object as measured from another moving reference frame.
teacher_note: Emphasize sign convention: if police behind thief, relative velocity positive means closing gap.
